coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of polyarylate thin-film nanocomposite membrane based on graphene quantum dots interlayer for enhanced gas separation performance
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Yuhui
Chen, Yuhao
Bao, Shanshan
Sun, Haixiang
Wang, Yaxuan
Ge, Baosheng
Li, Peng
Hou, Yingfei
coredata.dc:description: Interlayered-thin film nanocomposite (i-TFN) membranes with nanomaterials as the interlayer has attracted more and more attention of researchers in recent years due to the effective regulation of the separation layer structure in the membrane separation field. In this work, the amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized and applied as an interlayer to modify the polyethersulfone (PES) ultrafiltration substrate. Then interfacial polymerization (IP) between trimesoyl chloride (TMC) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) was performed to prepare novel i-TFN polyarylate membranes for CO2/N2 separation. The effect of the N-GQDs deposition amount on the structure and gas separation performance of the composite membrane was studied thoroughly. The results indicated that the i-TFN polyarylate membrane presented a particular sandwich structure and provided an effective transport channel for the diffusion of CO2. Moreover, the affinity of the N-containing groups (e. g. amino and amide groups) in the i-TFN membrane and the CO2 molecules helped to increase the CO2/N2 separation selectivity. At the optimal preparation condition, the obtained i-TFN membrane showed CO2/N2 selectivity of 23.3 with CO2 permeance of 174.5 GPU. This work proposes a novel nanomaterial interlayer to effectively improve the gas permselectivity by regulating the structure of i-TFN membranes, which has significant instruction for the preparation of high-performance i-TFN by exploring the interlayer of other nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyarylate membrane
Gas separation
Interfacial polymerization
Graphene quantum dots
Interlayer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of two kinds of quantum dots
Characterization of interlayer and i-TFN membranes
Gas permselectivity performance of i-TFN membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of N-GQDs.
Schematic diagram of i-TFN membranes prepared by interfacial polymerization.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of GQDs and N-GQDs, Raman spectra of (c) GQDs and (d) N-GQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of GQDs and N-GQDs, C1s scans of (b) GQDs and (c) N-GQDs, (d) O1s scans of GQDs and (e) N1s scans of N-GQDs.
TEM images of (a) GQDs and (b) N-GQDs.

SEM images of surface morphology of i-TFN membranes modified by different interlayer. (a) β-CD-TMC, (b) β-CD-TMC/GQDs-1, (c) β-CD-TMC/GQDs-3, (d) β-CD-TMC/GQDs-5, (e) β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs-1, (f) β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs-3 and (g) β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs-5. (a'-g') The cross-sectional SEM images of (a-g), respectively.
The interaction energy of GQDs-PES and N-GQDs-PES pair by molecular dynamics simulation.
Reaction mechanism diagram of β-CD, N-GQDs and TMC.
(a) ATR-FTIR spectra of the i-TFN membranes modified by different interlayers and (b) XPS spectrum of β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs with only 10 mL of N-GQDs solution suction-filtered as interlayer.
Water contact angles of the PES ultrafiltration membrane modified with (a) GQDs and (b) N-GQDs interlayer.
Effect of different interlayers on CO2/N2 separation performance of (a) β-CD-TMC/GQDs-x, (b) β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs-x i-TFN membrane (CO2/N2 = 1:1).
Schematic illustration of gas transport from the (a) pristine TFN membrane and (b) interlayered-TFN membrane.
The deposited amount of GQDs or N-GQDs per unit area of i-TFN membrane. (The membrane area is 12.56 cm2.).

row.entry: The volume of GQDs or N-GQDs solution (mL)
The deposited amount of GQDs or N-GQDs per unit area (g/cm2)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
Notes: The deposited amount of GQDs or N-GQDs per unit area of i-TFN membrane (L, g/cm2) can be calculated by the following formula:
L =
c ∙ V
S
Here, c is the solution concentration of GQDs (g/mL), V refers to the volume of suction filtration liquid (mL), and S means the membrane area of the suction filtration interlayer (cm2).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The content of elements in GQDs and N-GQDs.
Comparison of the permselectivity of β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs-3 membrane with other gas separation membranes fabricated by IP.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of polyarylate thin-film nanocomposite membrane based on graphene quantum dots interlayer for enhanced gas separation performance

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Interlayered-thin film nanocomposite (i-TFN) membranes with nanomaterials as the interlayer has attracted more and more attention of researchers in recent years due to the effective regulation of the separation layer structure in the membrane separation field. In this work, the amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized and applied as an interlayer to modify the polyethersulfone (PES) ultrafiltration substrate. Then interfacial polymerization (IP) between trimesoyl chloride (TMC) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) was performed to prepare novel i-TFN polyarylate membranes for CO2/N2 separation. The effect of the N-GQDs deposition amount on the structure and gas separation performance of the composite membrane was studied thoroughly. The results indicated that the i-TFN polyarylate membrane presented a particular sandwich structure and provided an effective transport channel for the diffusion of CO2. Moreover, the affinity of the N-containing groups (e. g. amino and amide groups) in the i-TFN membrane and the CO2 molecules helped to increase the CO2/N2 separation selectivity. At the optimal preparation condition, the obtained i-TFN membrane showed CO2/N2 selectivity of 23.3 with CO2 permeance of 174.5 GPU. This work proposes a novel nanomaterial interlayer to effectively improve the gas permselectivity by regulating the structure of i-TFN membranes, which has significant instruction for the preparation of high-performance i-TFN by exploring the interlayer of other nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane technology plays an increasingly important role in CO2 capture due to its low expense, ease of operation, and environmental compatibility [1-3]. However, there still exists enormous challenges to improve the performance of gas separation membranes because of the trade-off phenomenon between permeability and selectivity [4,5]. The mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) which consist of a dispersed phase of nanomaterials within polymeric matrix could surpass the trade-off limitations of the polymer membrane. Nanofillers, such as metal–organic frameworks (MOF) [6], graphene oxide (GO) [7] and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [8], etc., can regulate the chain structure of the polymer, introduce free volume and exert a tortuous path for gas transport across the membrane [9-13]. Nevertheless, due to the easy agglomeration of nanofillers in the polymer matrix, it is difficult for the MMMs to scale up in the industry [14]. Therefore, some smaller nanomaterials, such as graphene quantum dots have not even been used as fillers for the preparation of MMMs to separate CO2 and N2. Furthermore, the gas permeance is inversely proportional to the membrane thickness, thus how to increase the gas permeance without loss of selectivity by reducing the membrane thickness has received great attention [15,16].

Interfacial polymerization (IP) becomes a newly-developing method for preparing ultrathin gas separation membrane, which is a rapid self-inhibition reaction and easy to industrialize [17]. Up to now, the development of the thin film composite (TFC) membrane prepared by IP is mainly centered in the field of water treatment. The deployment of IP to produce gas separation membranes still lag behind [18-20], despite the fact that TFC membranes possess attractive characteristics for gas separation. Li et al. [21] reported a multi-permselective membrane with solubility, diffusivity and reactivity selectivity for CO2 separation by IP method. The separation selectivity of CO2/N2 mixed gas (15/85) was as high as 138 based on the comprehensive distinctions between CO2 and other light gases in size, condensability and reactivity. Yu et al. [22] fabricated ultrathin polyarylate (PAR) membranes by employing a twisted phenol monomer and attained significant CO2-permeance compared to other reported TFC membranes.

Currently, building an interlayer in the thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane to increase the permeance is widely used in the water treatment field. It is favorable to forming an ultrathin and defect-free selective layer, thereby enhancing the structural stability of interlayered-thin film nanocomposite (i-TFN) membrane [23]. Karan et al. [24] prepared ultrathin microporous PAR membranes through the IP process between phenol monomer and trimesoyl chloride (TMC). The cadmium hydroxide nanochains were introduced as the sacrificial layer to regulate the rate of IP reaction, with the result that the methanol flux of i-TFN membrane constructed on porous alumina support was 2 orders of magnitude higher than that of the commercial membrane. Zhao et al. [25] developed a novel stable azo-based interlayer to modify the polysulfone ultrafiltration membrane and performed a rapid in-situ coupling reaction between a dual diazonium salt and tannic acid. The resulting composite membrane showed 2.71 times increase in water flux compared with the conventional one. Similarly, the strategy of building interlayers can also be adopted to improve the permeance in gas separation membrane without loss of selectivity, even improve both in the meanwhile [26]. The construction key of the i-TFN membrane is to select appropriate interlayer materials.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), of which size is below 20 nm, are a new class of carbon nanomaterials and possess hydrophilic carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the edge of the nanosheets [27,28]. Compared with graphene derivatives, such as graphene oxide [11] and reduced graphene oxide [29], GQDs with a nearly zero-dimensional structure are conducive to exposing more oxygen-rich functional groups, which presents better hydrophilicity and dispersion in water. Moreover, GQDs have the advantages of good optical properties, low toxicity, environmental friendliness and low cost, which have broad application prospects in the fields of chemical sensing, nano-medicine and photo-catalysis [11,27-31]. Based on its excellent hydrophilicity, GQDs have been incorporated into the membranes to significantly enhance membrane permeability for water treatment [32-37]. Xu et al. [33] prepared a novel class of TFC membranes for forward osmosis (FO) desalination by incorporating GQDs into the polyethyleneimine (PEI) aqueous solution, which exhibited a highly hydrophilic and neutrally charged membrane surface, resulting in improved water flux and a comparable reverse salt flux. Xue et al. [34] developed a high flux nanofiltration (NF) membrane by means of the incorporation of GQDs in the aqueous solution. The results indicated that the pure water flux of the novel NF membranes increased with the increase of the GQDs concentration, which was almost 3.9 times higher than that of the pristine membrane.

On the other hand, amino groups can be introduced by the condensation reaction of the carboxyl groups on graphene quantum dots with ethylenediamine [38], which increases its application possibility. In theory, the affinity between the amino groups of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and CO2 molecules can enhance the reactivity selectivity of the TFN membrane. To the best of our current knowledge, there is no research on the incorporation of N-GQDs into the TFN membrane for gas separation.

In this work, β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) and TMC were used as aqueous and organic phase monomers respectively to form polyarylate separation layer by IP method. GQDs and N-GQDs were synthesized and used as interlayers to be suction-filtered on the polyethersulfone (PES) ultrafiltration membranes to develop novel i-TFN membranes. The effects of GQDs and N-GQDs amount on the microstructure and gas separation property of the composite membrane were discussed in detail.

The PES ultrafiltration membranes are commercial products with an average pore diameter of 15 nm. β-CD (AR) and TMC (AR) were provided by Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). N-hexane (AR), ethylenediamine (EDA, AR) and NaOH (AR) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The absolute citric acid (CA, AR) was bought from Shanghai Sann Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). The dialysis bag with a cut-off molecular weight of 3500 was supplied by Nanjing Senberga Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). All chemicals were analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The deionized (DI) water (conductivity less than 10 μs/cm, pH = 6.0) used in this research was self-produced in our laboratory.

GQDs were prepared by the pyrolysis of CA according to the “bottom-up” approach [39]. The schematic diagram of the pyrolysis principle of CA was illustrated in Fig. 1 . Because CA contains abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, dehydration and carbonization reactions can occur under heating conditions. 20 g of absolute citric acid was placed into a 50 mL beaker and heated at 200 °C for 1 h to obtain a brown-yellow liquid. After cooling to room temperature, a certain amount of water was added to dissolve it and dialyzed it with a dialysis bag for one day to remove unreacted citric acid and small molecule polycondensate. Finally, the obtained product was freeze-dried to obtain brown-yellow GQDs.

The GQDs attained through the above method and a predetermined amount of EDA were dissolved in water, mixed uniformly, and then heated in a 50 mL hydrothermal reaction kettle at 200 °C for 5 h. After the completion of the hydrothermal reaction, it was cooled to room temperature and dialyzed again with a dialysis bag for one day to remove unreacted EDA. Finally, the reaction liquid was freeze-dried to obtain N-GQDs (yellow-black solid).

According to our previous work [40], the pristine PES (p-PES) ultrafiltration support was pretreated by immersing in the DI water overnight, which can remove the impurities that may affect the experiment. After that, the air knife was used to remove the water attached on the PES membrane surface. GQDs or N-GQDs were dissolved in the deionized water, and their concentration was 0.01 w/v%. Then a certain amount of GQDs or N-GQDs solution was suction-filtered on the pre-treated PES substrate membrane, and the volume of solution was 10, 30, and 50 mL, respectively. The parallel modified membranes were designated as PES/GQDs-1, PES/GQDs-3, PES/GQDs-5 and PES/N-GQDs-1, PES/N-GQDs-3, PES/N-GQDs-5, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the i-TFN membranes were prepared on the modified PES substrate containing GQDs or N-GQDs interlayer through the IP process. Prior to IP, the aqueous phase solution was prepared by dissolving a certain amount of NaOH and β-CD in deionized water, and the concentration of β-CD and NaOH were 2 w/v% and 1 w/v%, respectively. The organic phase solution was prepared by dissolving a certain amount of TMC in n-hexane (1 w/v%). Next, the modified substrate membrane was fixed on an acrylic board frame and then 25 mL aqueous monomer was poured onto its surface. Residual aqueous phase solution was wiped off by air knife after 10 min immersion. Then, the membrane surfaces modified by β-CD were impregnated with 25 mL of the TMC/n-hexane solution for 10 min. Subsequently, the surplus solution was removed and the i-TFN membrane was post-treated at 50 °C for 10 min to enhance the cross-linking degree of the membrane while volatilizing the residual solution. The i-TFN membrane without the interlayer was named β-CD-TMC. The resultant GQDs and N-GQDs i-TFN membranes were designated as β-CD-TMC/GQDs-x and β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs-x, where x represented the volume of GQDs or N-GQDs solution on the supporting membrane. The interlayer deposited amount of GQDs or N-GQDs per unit area of the i-TFN membrane was shown in Table 1 .

The chemical structure of i-TFN membranes and two kinds of GQDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Escalab 250XL, USA) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, NicoletTM iS10 spectrometer, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X’ Pert-Pro MPD, PANalytical B.V., Netherlands) was used to characterize the structure of two different quantum dots by the peak positions of the X-rays. The microstructural characterization of GQDs and N-GQDs was performed by Raman spectroscopy (JY Labram HR 800, HORIBA Jobin Yvon, French). The average particle size of GQDs and N-GQDs was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G30, Philips-FEI, Netherlands). Then, the hydrophilicity of the two kinds of interlayers was also measured by means of a water contact angle analyzer (Kruss Gmbh DSA30, Germany) and the roughness was attained by Atom Force microscope (AFM, Shimadzu SPM-9700, Japan) The surface and cross-section morphologies of the membranes were attained by means of a field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S4800, Japan).

The self-regulating equipment depended on the concentration difference principle was used to measure the gas permeselectivity of the polyarylate i-TFN membranes, which was expounded in our earlier research [26]. The test diaphragm was held in a circular metal cell with 3.14 cm2 of the effective test area. The two-component gas mixture on the feed side originally comes from two cylinders containing CO2 and N2, respectively. When the test begins, the flow rate of each component gas was accurately controlled through two flowmeters, which can ensure that the gas mixture ratio was always 50/50 by the time reaching the membrane feed side. The mixed CO2/N2 (50/50 vol%) gas was blown into the i-TFN membrane and the permeate gas was purged into the gas chromatography (GC, SHMADZU, Japan) by the argon as the sweeping gas. Then, the component of permeate gas was identified by GC. The process maintained the stabilization of the concentration difference on both sides of the membrane and was carried out at 25 °C with a feed gas pressure of 1 bar. The calculation formula of gas permeance (Ri) is as follows:
(1)
R i
=
N i
Δ P × A
m o l
m
- 2
s
- 1
Pa
- 1
=
10
10
3.35
N i
Δ P × A
( G P U )

where Ni represented the permeate flow rate of component i (mol s−1), ΔP is the trans-membrane pressure difference of i (Pa), A is the effective area of the membrane (m2). The unit of Ri is in GPU (1 GPU = 10-6 cm3 (STP) cm-2s−1 cmHg−1 = 3.35 × 10-10 mol m−2 s -1 Pa−1). The measurement results were obtained through averaging the data at least three times. When the volume ratio of CO2/N2 is 50/50 vol%, the mole fraction ratio of the components in the feed gas mixture is 1(STP). The separation factor (αi/j) can be defined as follows:
(2)
Î± i/j
=
R i
R j
Where Ri and Rj represent the gas permeance of the two components, respectively.

The interaction forces between the PES substrate and the two kinds of quantum dots were calculated by molecular dynamics. The force field adopted was the COMPASS II force field that came with the Materials Studio software. Each model was structurally optimized before the calculation, and 1500 ps of dynamics calculations were performed after the optimization. After the simulation, the last 300 ps of the trajectory file were selected to analyze the results.

The interaction energy can be calculated by the following equation.
(3)
E
interaction
=
E
( P E S
+
q u a n t u m
d o t )
-
E
PES
-
E
quantum
d o t

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of two kinds of quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups of two kinds of quantum dots were investigated by FTIR spectra and the results are displayed in Fig. 3 (a). The absorption peaks at 3300 and 1730 cm−1 can be ascribed to the hydroxyl groups (–OH) and carboxyl groups (C = O), respectively, which confirms the existence of –COOH groups in the GQDs particles and CA [41]. The absorption peak intensity of hydroxyl groups in the spectrum of CA is larger than that of GQDs, demonstrating the dehydration and carbonation reaction of CA. The peak at 1642 cm−1 in GQDs is attributed to the C = C stretching vibration of the aromatic ring, indicating that the obtained GQDs contain the aromatic ring [33]. The peaks at 1665 cm−1 (C = O) and 1560 cm−1 (–NH-) are the characteristic groups of the amide group in N-GQDs, and the intensity of hydroxyl groups in N-GQDs declines obviously, which confirms the chemical reaction between GQDs and EDA.

XRD patterns and Raman spectra of GQDs and N-GQDs were illustrated in Fig. 3(b)-(d). A wide peak (002) at 2θ = 26° corresponding to an interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm indicates that carbonizing CA produces graphite structures [39]. The Raman spectra of both of GQDs and N-GQDs present G peak at 1600 cm−1, which is assigned to the vibration of the sp2 hybridization of graphitic carbon [42], and D peak at 1400 cm−1, which provides the evidence of the presence of structural defects and disorders in the graphite lattice produced by edges, vacancies, and epoxide and hydroxyl groups [43]. The peak intensity ratio of the D and G band (ID/IG) demonstrates the extent of surface defects [37]. N-GQDs appear a higher value of ID/IG (1.92) than that of GQDs (0.67), which indicates that there are more structural defects or disorders in N-GQDs nanoparticles. Such a change represents the successful occurrence of the amination reaction [44] and the amino groups in N-GQDs have an excellent affinity with CO2 molecules, which is favorable for improving the reactivity selectivity of the i-TFN membrane.

XPS was used to analyze the surface elemental composition of N-GQDs and the spectra were illustrated in Fig. 4 and Table 2 . The peaks at 285, 399, and 533 eV are ascribed to the binding energies of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively and the peak assigned to binding energy N1s appears in N-GQDs, which demonstrates the success of the amination reaction. According to the C1s XPS spectra, the peaks at 284.24, 286.00, 288.28, 284.50, and 286.69 eV are attributed to C-C, C-O, O-C = O, C-N, and N-C = O groups, respectively. Compared with GQDs, there are no O-C = O groups appearing in the spectrum of N-GQDs, which means that all carboxyl groups of GQDs are involved in the amination reaction, so the amination degree of N-GQDs is 100%. However, the peaks assigned to the C-N and N-C = O groups present based on the reaction of –COOH groups of GQDs with –NH2 groups derived from EDA. Furthermore, the content of C-N and N-C = O groups in N-GQDs is very close, indicating that the amine group at one end of the EDA molecule participates in the reaction, and the amine group at the other end is in a free state, which is conducive to improving the affinity between the membrane and CO2 molecules.

The element analysis results are shown in Table 2, which indicates that a new element N appears in the N-GQDs. The changes of the elemental content coincide with the structure of the target product, demonstrating the successful synthesis of amino-functionalized quantum dots.

The TEM images of GQDs and N-GQDs are displayed in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that GQDs and N-GQDs are dot-like and have a uniform particle size with the average size of GQDs and N-GQDs of 2.33 and 3.85 nm, respectively. The crystallographic spacing remains nearly constant after the amino-functionalized modification, indicating that the amination reaction does not change the structure of N-GQDs nanoparticles obviously. This may be very beneficial for preparing a uniform membrane surface layer compared with other nanomaterials based on the smaller particle size. In general, nanoparticles with a particle size of much higher than 50 nm used to fabricate the i-TFN membranes usually generate numerous defects between the nanoparticles and the selective separation layer [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of interlayer and i-TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: A series of structure characterizations were performed in order to investigate the impact of the interlayer on the IP process and the i-TFN membrane properties.

The SEM images of the surface morphology and cross-sectional thickness of i-TFN membranes modified by different amount of interlayers were shown in Fig. 6 . The pristine membrane exhibits a circular porous network structure which is completely different from that of the i-TFN membrane with interlayer modified and the reason could be attributed to the participation of the GQDs or N-GQDs during the IP process. The GQDs size is smaller than the average pore size of the PES substrate and a small amount of GQDs can pass through the substrate. However, as shown in Fig. S1, the porosity of the PES substrate is low so that most of the GQDs are interlaced and stacked to form a complete and uniform interlayer on the surface of the substrate, and the original pores are completely covered by the GQDs interlayer on the modified PES supporting. Moreover, comparing the cross-sectional image of the substrate after the N-GQDs suction filtration with the pristine one, it can be seen that there is a certain thickness of the nascent layer on the PES supporting membrane which indicates that the quantum dots with particle size be smaller than the pore size of the substrate are successfully prepared as the interlayer. The i-TFN membranes modified by two different interlayers demonstrate diverse surface morphologies. The surface of i-TFN membranes modified by GQDs interlayer exhibits a pleated structure and the fold size increases with the improvement of the amount of interlayer deposited. In order to demonstrate the structural change more clearly, the larger size SEM images are provided in Fig. S2. The reasons are described as follows. The β-CD molecules move perpendicular to the interlayer direction, and GQDs molecules promote the forward reaction of β-CD with TMC. When the deposition amount of GQDs is few, GQDs and β-CD diffuse together to the interface and undergo the IP process, and the β-CD-TMC membrane grows laterally to form a leaf-like structure. With the increase of GQDs deposition, the surface structure of the film changes from leaf-like to obvious fold structure. On the contrary, the β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs membranes present a relatively flat surface compared with that of β-CD-TMC/GQDs membranes as shown in Fig. 6, which has abundant little bumps. During the IP process, the strong interaction between N-GQDs and PES substrate slows down the diffusion rate of β-CD [45]. Moreover, with the increase of the N-GQDs amount, more aqueous phases are involved in the IP reaction, and the surface of the β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs membrane gradually forms obvious bumps from the original point structure. In addition, the surface roughness of the composite membrane is also enhanced, which is conducive to improving the gas permeability.

The cross-sectional image of the i-TFN membrane indicates that GQDs molecules completely diffuse to the top of the separation layer while N-GQDs molecules partially diffuse to the top resulting in the coverage of the polyarylate membrane produced by the reaction of β-CD with TMC. Molecular dynamics simulations were used to compare the interaction forces between the two interlayers and the substrate and the simulation model is shown in Fig. S3. As shown in Fig. 7 , the energy between PES substrate and N-GQDs is lower than that with GQDs, suggesting stronger interaction and higher adhesion in the N-GQDs-PES pair, which leads to the incomplete diffusion of N-GQDs compared with that of GQDs. Consequently, the β-CD-TMC/GQDs and β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs membranes obtained have completely different structures as shown in Fig. 6, which are named as top-interlayer and sandwich structure, respectively.

Combined with the above analysis, the selective layer of β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs with a sandwich structure can be divided into three layers. The surface layer and the bottom layer is N-GQDs interlayer material, and the middle layer is formed by the reaction of β-CD and TMC. From the below XPS results analysis, it can be concluded that β-CD, N-GQDs and TMC in the surface layer are involved in IP reaction, and the reaction principle is shown in Fig. 8 . The reason is that a small amount of N-GQDs on the surface of the interlayer have formed hydrogen bonds with β-CD, and then diffuse together to the interface. The amino groups in N-GQDs have more reactive than the hydroxyl groups of β-CD and react with TMC preferentially.

The cross-sectional SEM images indicate that the thickness of i-TFN membranes with GQDs and N-GQDs interlayer becomes thicker as the amount of interlayer deposited increases. In general, with the increase of hydrophilic interlayer deposited, more aqueous monomers β-CD would be maintained in the hydrophilic interlayer to form a dense separation layer at the beginning of the IP process, which prevents the diffusion of aqueous monomers and eventually leads to a decline in the thickness of the separation layer [46]. However, the hydroxyl group-based aqueous monomer β-CD employed for IP reaction in this work is less reactive than conventional diamines (e.g., piperazine and m-phenylenediamine). Therefore, a relatively loose polyarylate separation layer forms on the PES support film after the IP reaction, which is favorable for the β-CD molecules to diffuse into the organic phase and form a thicker separation layer. In addition, the reaction between GQDs or N-GQDs interlayer material and TMC can also increase the thickness of the separation layer due to the active groups of interlayer material.

The i-TFN film modified by N-GQDs is inferior to that modified by GQDs in the thickness of the separated layer, which is also confirmed by SEM images. This is mainly because of the different interactions between the interlayers and PES substrate, producing different transport resistance for aqueous phase monomers. With reference to Freger’s dynamic model for forming the selective layer of the TFC membrane, the following equation can be used to calculate the thickness of the separation layer [47].
(4)
Δ =
LD
k
C a
f a
+
C b
f b
1 3

where L (m) represents the thickness of the contact surface; D (m2 s−1) is the diffusivity of the aqueous phase monomer into the organic phase; k (L mol−1 s−1) is the rate of IP process; Ca (mol L-1) and Cb (mol L-1) represent respectively the balance concentrations of the aqueous phase and the organic phase monomer; fa and fb mean the functionality of the two-phase monomer. It is not hard to find that increasing the value of Ca and decreasing the value of D are essential to reducing the thickness of the active layer (δ). Through the above molecular simulation analysis, most N-GQDs molecules are attached to the PES substrate, and D is reduced because of the hydrogen bonding between N-GQDs and β-CD. However, almost all GQDs move to the surface of the i-TFN membrane, which makes β-CD more convenient to diffuse into the organic phase by hydrogen bonding with GQDs, that is, the value of D is increased. Therefore, the thickness of i-TFN membranes modified by N-GQDs is relatively thinner compared to that of GQDs modified. Meanwhile, the cross-linking degree of the polyarylate membrane would also increase due to the reduction of the diffusion rate of the aqueous phase monomer.

The surface functional groups of the i-TFN membranes were characterized by ATR-FTIR. As displayed in Fig. 9 (a), the peak at 1712 cm−1 is a stretching vibration of –COOC–, indicating the occurrence of the interfacial polymerization reaction between the chloride group of TMC and the hydroxyl group of β-CDs [26]. The peak of the O = C-Cl group of i-TFN membranes appearing at 1803 cm−1 has evidently reduced after deposition of amino-functionalized GQDs on the PES substrate, which only appears in the β-CD-TMC/GQDs-x and β-CD-TMC membrane. The reason is that more acyl chloride groups of TMC participate in the IP reaction in the β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs membrane. Such phenomenon indicates that the addition of the interlayer is capable of increasing the crosslinking degree of the membrane and it also supports our previous statement that the crosslinking degree of β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs is higher due to the reduction in the diffusion rate of the aqueous phase monomer. Moreover, the characteristic peak of O = C-N appears in the β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs, which demonstrates the achievement of the reaction between the amino groups of N-GQDs and the acid chloride group of TMC (Fig. S4).

The XPS spectrum of β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs with only 10 mL of N-GQDs solution suction-filtered as interlayer is shown in Fig. 9 (b). The peak at 399.55 eV is ascribed to the N-C = O group, indicating that the amino group of N-GQDs reacts with the acid chloride group of TMC to form the N-C = O group. The appearance of N-C = O provides strong evidence for our previous claim that the interlayer will diffuse to the top of the separated layer. This statement is based on the fact that the XPS detection depth is generally believed to be less than 10 nm.

As presented in Fig. 10 , the water contact angle of the substrate gradually decreases with increasing the deposition amount of the interlayer. The AFM results show that there is no significant change in the roughness of the PES substrate before and after the interlayer modification (Fig. S5), which indicates that the decrease in contact angle is mainly ascribed to the elevated hydrophilicity of the membrane surface [48]. The increase in hydrophilicity can be assigned to a large number of hydrophilic groups (e. g. hydroxyl and amine groups) on the interlayer. This result is also consistent with our previous statement that the substrate hydrophilicity is improved by the modification of the interlayer which will inevitably store more aqueous phase monomers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas permselectivity performance of i-TFN membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The gas permeance and selectivity of i-TFN membranes modified with different interlayers was evaluated by mixing CO2 and N2 (1:1) and the results are shown in Fig. 11 . Compared with the i-TFN membrane, the pristine PES substrate shows much higher gas permeance with an inferior separation factor. The reason is that the pore diameter (∼20 nm) of the substrate is much larger than the dynamic diameter of permeate gas, which leads to the CO2 and N2 molecules passing through the membrane simultaneously.

As shown in Fig. 11 (a), the permeance of both CO2 and N2 shows a downward tendency with increasing deposition of GQDs interlayer, and the selectivity of the i-TFN membrane has improved, which is following the trade-off principle of the composite membrane. The phenomenon is related to the increasing separation layer thickness discussed above. As the thickness of the i-TFN membrane increases, the gas transmission path becomes long and reduces the permeance of gases. Since the dynamic diameter of N2 (0.36 nm) is greater than that of CO2 (0.33 nm), the transmission resistance of N2 is higher than that of CO2, which results in the more obvious decrease in N2 permeance and improves the separation factor of the i-TFN membrane with the increase of the separation thickness [49]. Therefore, the selectivity of β-CD-TMC/GQDs membrane increases gradually with the increase of interlayer deposition amount. For the β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs membrane (Fig. 11(b)), the trend of the separation factor is similar to that of β-CD-TMC/GQDs membranes with increasing deposition of the interlayer, and the variation tendencies of CO2 permeances are not positively correlated with the thickness change.

Combined with the above analysis, its irregular changes are mainly attributed to the introduction of N-GQDs. The presence of N-GQDs promotes the improvement of the crosslinking degree of the separation layer. Due to the loose structure of the interlayer, the nanochannels have formed for the permeate into the surface pores, thereby the geometric restriction characteristic of two-layer structure has been weakened, without adding additional transport resistance (Fig. 12 ) [50]. In addition, some N-containing groups enhance the affinity of CO2 also contributes to the permselectivity improvement [51], and the mechanism has shown in S6.

As the suction-filtered amount of N-GQDs increases, the concentration of N-containing groups continues to augment in the i-TFN membrane. The N-containing groups provide many reactive selection sites for CO2 transport and promotes CO2 permeance while improving selectivity. Therefore, β-CD-TMC/N-GQDS-3 has a higher permeance and separation factor than β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs-1. However, the further increase of β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs-5 thickness impedes the transmission of CO2 molecules, resulting in a certain decrease in the permeance and the separation factor is almost unchanged (α difference of 1). Comprehensively, the optimal preparation condition is the suction-filtered amount of 30 mL of N-GQDs. The CO2 permeance of β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs-3 is up to 174.5 GPU with a relatively high CO2/N2 selectivity of 23.3.

The comparison of the separation performance of β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs-3 membrane with other gas separation membranes fabricated by IP published in the literature is shown in Table 3 . The results show that the i-TFN membrane (β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs-3) prepared by this method has better CO2 permeance and moderate selectivity compared with most gas separation membranes prepared by the IP method. It indicates that the IP and interlayer modification can be used to obtain the CO2 gas separation membrane with excellent permeance and selectivity.

In this work, a novel polyarylate i-TFN membrane with a special sandwich structure was prepared based on the N-GQDs interlayer by the IP process of β-CD and TMC for CO2/N2 separation. The interlayer evidently affected the structure of the composite membrane by regulating the IP process, which in turn improved the gas separation performance of the i-TFN membrane. At the optimal deposition amount of N-GQDs interlayer, the CO2/N2 selectivity of the β-CD-TMC/N-GQDs membrane was as high as 23.3 with 174.5 GPU of CO2 permeance based on the thinner separation layer and the affinity between the amino groups of N-GQDs and CO2 molecules. This work proposes the mechanism of the nano-interlayer on the composite membrane structure and explains the way of gas transmission through the composite membrane, which is of great significance to the preparation of thin-film nanocomposite materials. Together the excellent gas separation performance and the facile method would make the new i-TFN membrane be applied in the industrialization field.

Yuhui Niu: Data curation, Validation, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yuhao Chen: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Shanshan Bao: Data curation, Validation, Investigation. Haixiang Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Validation, Supervision. Yaxuan Wang: Data curation, Visualization. Baosheng Ge: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Peng Li: Data curation. Yingfei Hou: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2022.121035.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Tang, D. Lu, C. Wu, Cation-assisted interactions between N-heterocycles and CO2, Phys. Chem. Phys. 17 (2015) 15725-15731.
